summary: O'Neil argues that while public outrage over NSA surveillance is justified, the lack of similar concern about private data collection by companies like Acxiom represents a dangerous blind spot. She explores why people fear government snooping more than corporate surveillance, despite private companies having significant power to deny jobs, loans, and insurance based on collected data. The post presents several theories for this asymmetry, including that people focus on worst-case scenarios with government but underestimate corporate harms, and that it's harder to notice when opportunities are quietly denied. O'Neil concludes by calling for the privacy debate to expand beyond government surveillance to include corporate data practices and time limits on personal data retention.
key_points: Private companies like Acxiom buy and sell more specific and potentially damaging personal information than the metadata government claims to collect
While government surveillance worst-case means imprisonment, corporate surveillance increasingly determines access to jobs, loans, mortgages, and insurance
People tend to notice rights being taken away but not opportunities being quietly denied, making corporate harms less visible
The U.S. government has actually partnered with Facebook and Google to prevent stronger European privacy laws
Those most harmed by private data collection are people who don't know to protect themselves and get funneled into bad options
money_quotes: What gets to me are the countless articles that make a big deal of Facebook or Google sharing private information directly with the government, while never mentioning that Acxiom buys and sells from Facebook on a daily basis much more specific and potentially damning information about people.
It begs the question, at least to me, of why we distrust the government with our private data but we trust private companies with our private data.

When private companies are spying on you, they don't have the power to put you in jail. They do increasingly have the power, however, to deny you a job, a student loan, a mortgage, and life insurance.
It's hard to know why you get denied a job, especially if you've been getting worse and worse credit card terms and conditions over the years. In general it's hard to notice when things don't happen.
I'm not getting denied a mortgage any time soon. It's the people who don't know to protect themselves, don't know to opt out, that I'm down-scored and funneled into bad options that I worry about.
I for one do not wish mistakes my children make when they're impetuous teenagers to haunt them when they're trying to start a family.
key_insight: The privacy debate's exclusive focus on government surveillance obscures the more immediate and widespread harms caused by private data brokers who quietly determine people's access to economic opportunities.
